section_title: INITIAL SETUP AND BASELINE INTRODUCTION
content:
Folks, good morning. We are conducting a latency test; audio is quick. I am not using the problematic
headset this morning and working with a single OBS scene to prevent audio loss, which has happened
twice before. I, as a complete noob with OBS, previously failed to assign an audio input capture to all
scenes, causing audio loss when switching charts. To prevent this, I am now using only one scene that I
know has audio, relying on the internal microphone for now. Today marks the first of three Baseline
measurements for your learning this year, as we approach one month since starting. I was out of
commission last week battling the flu, so I apologize if I cough during this presentation.

section_title: THE DRAWDOWN MITIGATION EXPERIMENT
content:
The first of three baseline measurements for you as the student this year is to observe your ability to
capture, regain, and mitigate drawdown. I am going to encourage you to go into a drawdown. This is a
method for you to determine your growth. There is no better way to determine how much you have learned
than by being put in a laboratory experiment. The measurement of understanding how to recapture or
mitigate drawdown is crucial. As a trader, whether in demo, paper, or a live funded account, you will
invariably make mistakes and do something incorrectly.

section_title: PURPOSE OF THE EXPERIMENT AND TRADING MINDSET
content:
At 9:30, we will be looking at live price action, but first, I want to build the foundation for today's experiment.
In my observation, no one teaches an approach to intentionally go into drawdown with the purpose of
correcting it. Everyone teaches how to chase money, enter trades, and build equity. While trying to make
money is why we are here, remember that everything I teach is by means of paper trading or demo trading.
I am not a licensed financial advisor, so I cannot give advice on live funds. Trading with live funds incurs
taxation and monetary law, removing the problem of measuring your ability and attitude as a student when
you are training and studying on your own.

section_title: NAVIGATING VOLATILE WEEKS AND FINANCIAL PREPAREDNESS
content:
Many of you try to measure your skill set way too early. At the beginning, with this first baseline, you may
fail this laboratory experiment this week; in fact, it's almost certain you will. We have Non-Farm Payroll
conditions, and the Fed Chairman will be testifying tomorrow and Wednesday. I purposely chose this week
to simulate how you might trade if you were rushing into real money trading before you should, perhaps to
pass a funded account challenge. Such pressures will push you to make decisions, which is why you
shouldn't trade with real money before having a nest egg of two years' living expenses in the bank. This
removes the pressure to profit from every trade, especially if you are not a seasoned technician. The trap in
this industry is the 'get rich quick' or 'trade for a living before you're ready' mentality. There's a right way to
do this.

section_title: SIMULATING FUNDED ACCOUNT DRAWDOWN
content:
To help you understand where you are in your learning right now, with limited experience, we will
encourage you to use a paper trading account on TradingView, which is free. I previously did a poll asking if
people would use funded account companies due to a lack of speculation capital. Most people preferred
the lower-tier funded accounts because they are cheaper. We will use the argument that eventually, you
would want to graduate to the highest tier and potentially manage multiple accounts. Based on my limited
experience with funded accounts (I don't personally trade them), a $150,000 account from a widely
followed company often has a maximum daily loss threshold.

section_title: THE DRAWDOWN RECOVERY ASSIGNMENT
content:
We are going to inspire you to simulate that daily maximum loss this week, specifically today. Before you
close your charts today, the laboratory experiment requires you to have already created a TradingView
paper trading account (if you haven't already). Set your account to $150,000. Your task today is to
intentionally draw that account down by $3,000. Try not to go more than $3,000, getting as close as you
can to $3,000, so your account should reflect around $147,000. Once you have that, your assignment is to
get back to $150,000 before Friday's close, but you cannot drop below $144,000. If you hit $144,000 at any
time, you have failed the assignment. Do not be afraid to fail. The whole purpose of this is as a baseline
measurement for your learning this year.

section_title: LIVE PRICE ACTION INTRODUCTION
content:
- NEW WEEK OPENING GAP
Let's switch over to live price action. This is the New Week Opening Gap (NWOG) for this week. I will be
posting a teaching on the NWOG principle on my YouTube channel soon, which I had meant to do last
Wednesday. Currently, we've created a short-term high, and price has drawn back down below that NWOG.
It didn't take out the sell-side below, but we have taken out the buy-side here. I am watching the Dollar
Index (DXY) and can now toggle through charts without losing audio, thanks to the OBS fix. This is live
data, folks, with only a small, seconds-long delay.

section_title: ES 5-MINUTE CHART ANALYSIS AND MARKET EXPECTATIONS
content:
Let's go to a five-minute chart with ES (E-mini S&P 500 futures). You are never really trying to rush,
especially today, because we've had a nice big range expansion in the daily chart on Friday. With the Fed
Chairman speaking tomorrow and Wednesday, there isn't likely to be a lot of animation in the market today.
This doesn't mean you can't participate, study, and observe what price is doing, but you must keep your
expectations within reason.

section_title: INTERMARKET ANALYSIS AND INITIAL PRICE OBJECTIVES
content:
Let's look at why ES might go back down into the New Week Opening Gap. We've taken buy-side here.
Now, observe the Dollar Index. We've taken a sell-side liquidity pool with a small Fair Value Gap (FVG)
there. I'm watching if DXY respects that FVG or goes through it. I'm not interested in doing anything in the
first few minutes of a day like today; just because the market opens doesn't mean we rush to place a bet
that's gambling. I'll periodically toggle through charts; the right side is the US cash CFD (not tradable in the
US), and the left is the March contract for ES. I'm watching the relationship between the lows. If ES gets to
a high, the minimum upside objective, studying price right now, would be 4077. I'm watching to see if down
- closed candles will support price and expand continuously up, not trying to go lower, which indicates
institutional order flow. You don't need ladders, depth of market, trend lines, or magnitude data; it's simply
reading price action itself.

section_title: ONE-MINUTE CHART ANALYSIS AND DXY CORRELATION
content:
Dropping down to a one-minute chart, if you look at this little Fair Value Gap, we've traded down into it. This
down-closed candle, as I mentioned, we want to see support price and look for expansion. If it goes above
here, we want to see it run into that 4077 level. As long as we are above the New Week Opening Gap this
morning, we side with being bullish. We would want to see the Dollar Index fail to go higher and instead
trade lower. For those asking how to add the DXY chart on the bottom, click the 'compare symbol' button,
select 'DXY', and then adjust to Candlestick chart and an all-black color scheme. We want to see DXY take
out its sell-side here and allow ES to expand to the upside. This is all tape reading; I'm not telling you to buy
it, and you shouldn't be doing anything with your paper trading account yet.

section_title: INTERMARKET DIVERGENCE AND CHALLENGING MARKET CONDITIONS
content:
Looking at NASDAQ, we've had a small run above that short-term high on ES. NASDAQ has gone below
its previous low, while ES has not seen that lower low. This relationship, where NASDAQ finds its way lower
but ES doesn't, indicates that ES is relatively stronger, suggesting it's the leader if everything moves to the
upside. The Dollar Index is not showing a strong desire to take out its sell-side yet. I will be trading in front
of you this year, showing entries, pyramiding, and hitting targets, with something special planned for
December. These are notes for a day like this, driven by the economic calendar. With the Fed Chairman
testifying the next two days and NFP conditions this week, it's going to be a very difficult week to navigate.
There's no shame in saying that; anyone claiming it's easy after the fact is misleading. Everything is
stacked against us as traders.

section_title: FUTURE LIVE TRADING AND VOLUME IMBALANCE
content:
All these consecutively down-closed candles are equivalent to a five-minute down-closed candle. Notice
how from the opening, this candle draws out in time into another discount array, which is a volume
imbalance. A volume imbalance occurs when there is a separation of candle bodies where there is no
overlapping. If bodies don't meet or overlap, it means there's a volume imbalance, and price is more likely
to return to that level. Ideally, you want to see a body laid across it, but in a fast market, it's not strictly
necessary. So far, the Dollar Index is still finding difficulty getting below its intraday low, and ES hasn't done
much in terms of pricing.

section_title: DAILY TRADING STRATEGY AND MARKET IMPULSES
content:
How would I coach you on utilizing a day like today? We want to wait the first 30 minutes, then trade inside
that 10 o'clock hour, studying what that first macro will do between 9:50 and 10:10. A macro is algorithmic
and will cause the market to run for liquidity or an inefficiency. By then, if the market hasn't done much, it
usually tips its hand, indicating what it will likely do going into the lunch hour (11 o'clock today). Looking at
the low and high, the halfway point is one standard deviation here (negative one). I want to see if it has the
willingness to get to this level. We have to measure the speed at which price gets to this level, as how we
trade this level indicates how we will trade once we get into the NWOG, and the rest of the above price.
One of the hardest things as a trader is the impulse to do something when you don't know what you're
looking to engage with. What is your premise for trading today? It needs to be clear and rooted in strong
foundations.

section_title: BEHAVIOR AT HIGHS AND DXY STAGNATION
content:
Looking at the current price, there's nothing except the initial few minutes trading down into a five-minute
Fair Value Gap. We outlined this, and price traded down to it. But look at how we're behaving at the highs.
Is this a market showing a willingness to expand aggressively after taking out the short-term high (where
buy stops would be)? Buy stops resting above the short-term high have been engaged multiple times, but
there's no willingness to run higher. Has it shown a willingness to break and reject that high, like a turtle
soup false breakout, and collapse below the Fair Value Gap? Not yet. This is where it's difficult for me as an
educator to convey how that impulse to 'do something' and the fear of missing out (FOMO) on the first run
of the morning can be managed. If we're not part of a clear trend, it feels like you've been cheated. This
FOMO will go away this year as you learn to control your impulses. I'm watching how price attempts a
higher high, but observing DXY, the same lower prices are not occurring. Both should be dancing together,
with perfect symmetry. If ES is expanding higher, DXY should indicate risk-on by posting lower prices. Is
DXY doing that yet? No, it's stagnant.

section_title: SELF-CONTROL AND OPENING RANGE DEFINITION
content:
This means we are in a very lethargic opening range. The opening range is the first 30 minutes of trading
from 9:30 to 10:00, always the same. We need a reference for where we closed last time. On Friday, we
closed here (regular trading session hours), then we opened up here, rallied, and ran that buy-side. But we
haven't seen confirmation from the Dollar Index. DXY has not made the lower low we would expect,
indicating it's likely to pull back down into this Opening Range Gap (ORG). The ORG is always at the 9:30
opening bell. It's the difference between the very first opening price and where we closed prior. You can
define this with rectangles or lines; it's a matter of personal choice.

section_title: TYPES OF GAPS (NDOG, NWOG, ORG)
content:
This is the Opening Range Gap (ORG). That is not the New Day Opening Gap (NDOG), which is the
difference between the closing price at 5 PM and the opening price at 6 PM. The New Week Opening Gap
(NWOG) is the difference between last Friday's closing price and Sunday's first opening price. So, there are
three specific gaps I teach, and you'll learn more about them. This ORG occurs every day at the 9:30
opening bell, not that there's always a gap, but when there's a separation between where we stopped
trading the regular session and where we open for the current day, that's the Opening Range Gap.

section_title: PATIENT TRADING AND TRUSTING THE PROCESS
content:
Let's go back into electronic trading hours and add the ORG to our chart for reference. This is free,
although you need real-time data, which I'm showing. You can toggle through your charts on a delay basis
and see what I'm showing you here. But there's nothing indicating a trade; no entry would have been
formed by now. One of the common questions I receive is 'How do I trust and not fear missing a move?'
The whole point of this year with you, in real-time data, is to show there's no impending doom, no
emergency, no rush to do something right now because you fear missing the big move. There are always
moves. This comes from trusting that the things you're learning repeat. Even if a nice, clean move takes
place that you didn't participate in (which we haven't seen an indication of yet), it doesn't matter. That trade
is one of many that will be forming in your entire career as a speculator or student of the market. You will
see lots of price action movements you weren't a participant to. No trader is in every move; you have to
sleep, eat, spend time with family, or get ill, like I was last week. So, you have to give yourself permission.

section_title: DXY SOFTNESS AND PROLONGED PATIENCE
content:
I'm watching to see if ES respects this order block and presses down into the sell-side here. Will it make
another attempt to go higher? The Dollar Index looks a little softer in here. So, in my mind, if the dollar was
going to go lower, it would be now. DXY is approaching its sell-side liquidity pool. I don't usually annotate
this much, but I'm doing it now for education to help you see what I'm referring to. The sell-side is cell stops
resting below an old relative equal low. We've been trading for 22 minutes, and not much has happened
yet, which is another lesson. You learn tape reading for months, not one week, thinking you're ready to
demonstrate or trade a small live account. What you learn by doing that is how to be fearful of losing and
be emotionally attached to the outcome. That's what trading with a live account, even with a small amount,
does before you know how to trade or read the marketplace. Anyone who tells you different is giving bad
advice. It's a perfect recipe to make you afraid of trading, afraid of the next setup, even when you take a
losing trade, because you have not properly conditioned or desensitized yourself to the outcome of the
trade.

section_title: AVOIDING FEAR-BASED TRADING
content:
By trading with live money when you don't know how to trade consistently, you prepare your mind to be
fearful of the outcome, which is the number one task as a trader: to overcome that fear. You have to remove
it. This is how you do it: you study price action to the point where seeing the setups is boring to you. You
also condition yourself to know what it's like when it's not likely to make a big move, preventing regret. So,
I'm watching to see how we react if price trades below this low here for the sell-side on the dollar, and how
we take this high or if they keep that high in place.

section_title: ECONOMIC CALENDAR INFLUENCE AND HIGH VS. LOW RESISTANCE
content:
From a market symmetry stance, if we take this low out, everything is balanced. This is not a magical
market. The market would have to make a serious momentum move to get things going this morning;
otherwise, it's probably going to trade directionless. Let's hypothetically refer to our economic calendar.
Tomorrow morning and on Wednesday morning, the Fed Chairman Powell is testifying. Because of that,
many folks have their hands in their pockets, waiting to see how the chips fall over the next two days.
Gamblers, high-energy caffeine junkies are out there always trying to do something, telling you to trade and
gamble in such environments because you don't know the market's influence. These same people often
have bad performance on those days. Individuals interested in learning self-control and measured risk do
not rush in these environments. They sit back and wait because it's the first mouse that gets clipped by the
mousetrap; the second mouse gets the cheese. You don't want to be the first mouse. Just let the market tip
its hand. We have plenty of time this month, this week, this year, to see amazing price runs.

section_title: DIFFERENTIATING HIGH AND LOW RESISTANCE CONDITIONS
content:
You're seeing that there are certain price runs I'm not personally interested in. You can't appreciate that if I
just say, 'I'm looking for low resistance liquidity run signatures.' What does that even mean? That's what
you're learning. You're learning what it's like to be in high resistance. It would be one thing to say, 'Okay, I'm
only going to sign on and do live streams in low resistance liquidity run conditions.' You'll see me call the
market, it'll run right off into my targets, barely have any drawdown from where I'm calling. But you learn
nothing from that because you will not know how to identify what it feels like, what it looks like, what the
conditions are that present High Resistance Liquidity Runs. I see several people, likely because they don't
watch or listen to all my content, missing the characteristics I've mentioned that lead to a high resistance
liquidity run condition. Yes, the market is moving, it can go to targets, but in those conditions, I opt out. I
don't want to participate. You're going to learn to participate in those only to get an appreciation for what it's
like when you're not in them. When you're not in a high resistance liquidity run condition, it means the
market wants to quickly run from one pool of liquidity to the next, or one premium array to a discount array,
and it does so efficiently and quickly. It doesn't mess around like what you're seeing here. This is all high
resistance liquidity conditions. The market is in no hurry. In plain terms, write in your journal: 'In high
resistance liquidity run conditions, the market is in no hurry to get to an opposing discount or premium
array,' because liquidity or inefficiency is encapsulated by that idea. Whereas in an environment like a low
resistance liquidity run, it's in a hurry, very quickly, to get to where it wants to go. That's what I'm training
you, and what I teach my students to look for in price action. It helps filter out the environments that most
likely will give you choppy days, where most traders get beat up.

section_title: HIGH RESISTANCE EXAMPLE AND STOP LOSS MANAGEMENT
content:
I'll give you an example of what high resistance is in this very moment. ES has traded down to our five
- minute Fair Value Gap, which entered into sell-side liquidity (sell stops below here). Who would have a sell
stop there? Anyone trying to go long who puts their stop right below, doing what retail books and educators
tell you: 'Put your stop loss to break even.' I don't teach that. Eventually, it will get there, but it needs to go
to first partial and expand for that to occur. We're not in a rush to move the stop loss; we rely on partials to
do the most heavy lifting. It's not important to move your stop loss too early because you won't know where
to put it in the beginning. This style buffers and provides a cushion, allowing experience to dictate when
you, the speculator, using your model, will move your stop loss, reduce risk, or make it break even. Every
trader grows at their own pace. My teaching style helps because it takes the worry out of the stop loss. The
stop loss is being paid to do a job; if price hits it, it removes you from more risk. You've already paid it good
money. So, don't worry about getting stopped out. If you get stopped out, you made a human error, a
misguided judgment; it's one transaction, like a flat tire on the way to work. It's the cost of doing business.

section_title: THE IMPORTANCE OF TAKING PARTIALS
content:
We're not looking for price simply to run in my favor and remove risk entirely. We're studying when we're in
a trade, waiting to see price get to an opportunity for us to take something off. So many people in this
industry, with large followings, constantly harp on taking partials as the stupidest thing. I've never
understood that argument because you don't know if your model and execution will yield a profitable exit if
you only accept your final target at terminus, and that's it; it's all or nothing. As an educator, it would be
foolish to say, 'Hold for your full target or nothing at all.' Firstly, it's not encouraging when you get stopped
out, especially if you had an opportunity to take something off. A partial is like your white belt. When you
hook up a trade, you're a white belt; when it allows you to take something off, that's your green or yellow
belt. Your second partial is your blue or brown belt, and then your terminus is your black belt. You've arrived
on that one trade, but your next trade, you start back at a white belt again. You need to have a place to take
something off because a partial profit has never, ever, ever failed in making money. It has a 100% strike
rate. That's perfect trading, folks. Partials pay every single time.

section_title: MANAGING GREED AND ACCEPTING MARKET REALITY
content:
You don't know, and the people who pontificate about how partials are not good, they don't know if their
next trade, or the one they're in right now waiting for a full target, is going to go there. You don't know that.
So you're opening yourself up for full risk, whatever the initial risk was, only hoping to reduce the risk but
never take anything. Think about what goes on in trading if traders are in a retail mindset. I'm watching to
see if we expand to the upside here because the dollar has broken here, so ES should start to gravitate up
into that NWOG. The idea of taking something off partially is that you are paying yourself. You put yourself
in the charts, you spent time, emotion, energy, mental equity, stressing. When the market presents an
opportunity to take something off and get paid, are you going to work half the week, and then illness takes
you out the rest of the week, and tell your boss, 'Listen, I couldn't finish the whole week, so just keep what
you paid me because I didn't give you my full week, so you don't owe me anything'? No one does that. If
you listen to these people in this industry tell you that partials are stupid, they'll never do it. Their results will
tell you that what they're saying is denial. They're not getting full targets; they're getting stopped out and
losing a lot more than they would if they just took partials. I have illustrated this many times, even in my
tape reading a couple of weeks ago. The markets are very fickle and difficult right now, not due to
algorithms changing, but just being constrained.

section_title: PARTIALS AND TRADING PSYCHOLOGY
content:
Because of that, if it was not for me saying this is where you would take a partial, this is a partial here, your
charts say this is a screenshot partial here, I'm conditioning you to do what you're trying to learn how to do,
which is to profit. What the books and other yahoos on social media teach is to hold for your target or that
you don't have conviction about your model. No, my model says get out with money; get out where I can
take money where it offers it to me. If your model says 'I'm always right or I get nothing and get stopped
out,' that's stupid to me, and it should be stupid to you as a listener. Who wants to put all that time, open
themselves up to risk, and when they had an opportunity to get paid like right now, this would be a perfect
opportunity? If you had gone long on that Fair Value Gap or traded into this down-closed candle, this
movement here, that's five handles. This would be a perfect opportunity, even though it hasn't hit the New
Week Opening Gap, to do what? Take a partial, because you don't know if a 'bomb' is going to drop
somewhere, or some craziness goes off in the country or the world that causes this to reverse and go
harshly in the other direction. You don't know that. I don't know that. So when I educate, when I teach, I
teach my students not to be worrisome or worried about that stop loss. It's there to do its job. Your focus is
watching: does it go to a level that allows you to do what? Get paid. If you can get paid, that's what makes
your bottom line go up. Not being perfect, not that your trade goes to target and no partial is taken. That is
such a hard task for a new student or a new trader to arrive at. So it makes no difference whether or not,
you know, the way I teach, it matters not if your targets are reached ultimately, because you're trying to do
what? Add more to your bottom line. And when the market presents these opportunities to do that, unless
you take a partial, the market's not going to do it for you. And there's been many times, and you've seen it a
few weeks back, where I openly gave you my limit order where I thought I was going to see an exit, and the
spread between the bid and ask on S&P did not let my limit order tag the actual high of the candle, which
was where my limit was, and it pulled back against me and I didn't get my final balance of my contract. You
don't know if your target's going to hit. But when the market's presenting a billboard sign flashing, 'Here's
some money, here's some money right here, all you got to do is scale out something that you are in a
position of,' but you don't want to do that because greed. And I'm teaching you how to manage greed by
taking something off at logical positions and places in price. And by doing that, over time, you condition
yourself and remove the whole necessity in your mind right now you think, 'I have to be right. I have to be
right, if I'm right, I'm a good trader.' No. You're a good trader if you manage risk, and you stick to rules, and
you place profits on that bottom line when it's made available to you. Targets are just the best-case
scenario. There's nothing, absolutely nothing, that states that mine, yours, or anybody else's in terms of
targets are ever going to be reached. We don't know that. We're submitting to the idea, the likelihood, but
nothing's for certain, especially today in these markets. These markets are extremely difficult.

section_title: ES, DXY, AND TAKING PARTIALS
content:
So, when we dropped below these relative equal lows here, that was sell stops being hit, also trading into
the Fair Value Gap on a 75-minute chart. What else was it dropping into? The Opening Range Gap.
Remember, I showed you the difference between where we traded and closed on Friday; that's Friday's
close, and this is where we opened up this morning at 9:30. That Opening Range Gap. We opened here
and traded down into it. It can have a complete closure of the gap, a half closure, or a one-quarter closure.
What did it do this morning? One quarter. It could have gone to the half; it didn't. And it showed what? It
showed price stacking up and showing a willingness to do what I put in a chart training: 'Because we want
to see it trade up into this area here, we want to see down-closed candles support price.' I initially said it
incorrectly; more specifically, 'we want to see down-closed candles supporting price.' We've seen the Fair
Value Gap here, the short-term high is taken. It trades back down into a down-closed candle. Is that
potentially a bullish order block? Does this support price? At the mean threshold, which is the midpoint of
that down-closed candle, is price being supported in that level there? You visually see it: yes, it does. At the
same time, what were we looking for in the Dollar Index? In the Dollar Index, we were looking for that sell
- side liquidity pool here, and we wanted to see price move lower through this low. If it does this, that means
it's going to cause risk-on. Risk-on is going to allow for ES to trade up. Trade up into what? The next New
Week Opening Gap. The next NWOG is up here, so 4077. Do we need 4077 to be traded to? Why?
Because we had already outlined a level before reaching that with our Fibonacci that would indicate this is
a fair place to expect a partial to be taken, and not have to see that level be taken out or traded to. This is
the magnet on price action, this range up here between 4077 and 4084, even. But because we're below it,
the first threshold is the low of the next New Week Opening Gap in terms of relationship to where we are in
price action. And the next one's here. So, let me check my other charts.

section_title: BULLISH ORDER BLOCK AND REAL-TIME LEARNING
content:
My son, Cameron, is with me today; some of you probably think I'm running a ventriloquist act. Now,
because of where we're trading right now from that Fair Value Gap, as I mentioned, we want to see the
down-closed candles support price. We see that. I'm going to annotate the five-minute bullish order block,
and then we'll drop down into a one-minute chart. Some of you that are new, young, or impatient, just want
to be doing something. There are other YouTubers pushing buttons and taking risk in front of you; go watch
them. I like some of them. But if you're trying to learn how to read price action the way I do it and teach my
students who are making big money with it, this is how you do it. There is no other way. No video I can
produce, no book I can write (even 20 books), will teach you this unless you are seeing it happen real-time
and it's being explained what it looks like. You won't understand it from static charts, hindsight, or even
market replay, because you have the benefit of knowing in market replay. That's why market replay reports
and backtesting showing equity curves are 'form fitted' you have that benefit of knowing. Whereas
watching this, you have no idea where these candles are painting, but I'm drawing your attention to specific
things: why it should do certain things and what I'm measuring it against. I don't show any indicators, but
I'm looking at the relationship between the Dollar Index and ES, and other charts across my eight monitors,
relating their information to this. Look at the difference between a price run like this. Yes, we ran to the Fair
Value Gap, went down inside the Opening Range, had displacement above the short-term high, and
watched if the buy-side wanted to expand. It was stalling here, then we had to take our attention to the
Dollar Index. We were watching and monitoring DXY. Everything became focal to the dollar: 'Does the
dollar want to go down below its low?' which would have been this here. We've gone through that. We were
looking for a willingness to go through it and not have any interest in going back above it. We want to see a
drive down into it. If it does that, then we can trust that this New Week Opening Gap will still be a draw on
liquidity. But do we just simply buy it and hold for it to trade to? You can if you want to be like everybody
else and trade with stress when you're not really versed yet as a speculator.

section_title: CONFIRMATION, TARGETS, AND SMT DIVERGENCE
content:
Versus the order block, down-closed candles are supposed to support price, and it does so. Every down
- closed candle doesn't make it an order block, but if it trades down into it, we want to see it trade not below
the mean threshold, which is the middle of the range of this candlestick. It traces down into here, then we
start to rally. We come back down, touch it again. I mentioned while we were trading just like this, in this
candle, 'Okay, it should expand higher, gravitating towards this New Week Opening Gap.' Why? Because I
took your attention down into the Dollar Index. We've already hit the 4077. Look at the high right here, up in
the upper part of the screen. This candle's high is exactly that low, 4077, so 4077 and a half. So we want to
watch and observe, does it gravitate to the consequent encroachment, which is the midpoint between the
low of the New Week Opening Gap and the high that you make of this alone right here? Done. 80% of your
trade should be off. So partials should have been taken in here. And 80% off, you then at that time, your
stop could roll to break even or something better than break even. It's up to you as to what that would be. In
my opinion, your stop should be just about below the midpoint of this range of price action. Equilibrium
would be right about here, so your stop should be here for the remaining 20%. Now, everything I just
outlined probably bored a lot of you that were impatient and just want to see something spectacular. But I
would have cut my own arm off in the 1990s to have someone outline what I just outlined to you: looking for
what it is I'm specifically aiming for in price, what do I trust to know that price is going to continuously drive
to that level, what factors am I weighing, what am I looking for, what do I not want to see in price action,
and everything that would support the idea is an inside price action, it's not an indicator. So now let's take a
quick look at these lows here. We're going to look at that in the NASDAQ. So the low formed at 9:30.
NASDAQ made a lower low here. Let's look at the YM (Dow). I don't trade the Dow, but I use it for SMT. So
here's 9:30. What do we see there? See that low right here? This low here, in relationship to that low, we
have a higher low on the Dow. And ES, we have a lower low comfortably, see that? As it trades into Fair
Value Gaps. So this Fair Value Gap, we talked about that real time. And we wanted to see it indicate to us,
even though we've taken out buy-side here, at that time, when we're in a slow market. A slow market is a
market that is predisposed to have adversities because of the economic calendar. In plain terms, Fed
Chairman Powell is going to talk tomorrow, Tuesday morning, and on Wednesday. Because of that, there's
a lot of traders and money that's not being put to risk yet. That means they're not trying to speculate.
Because of that, the market's going to have generally the tendency to create small ranges, small
fluctuations in price action. And you want to be very nimble and not try to marry the idea of a market that's
blasting off and having 120 handles up or 120 handles down. A big move today is not likely, whereas
tomorrow, because of his testifying, that invites the opportunity for market makers to exaggerate price
delivery. So we expect big movement tomorrow in the 10 o'clock hour, a lot of volatility in that hour on
Wednesday and on Tuesday. This morning is pretty much, in my opinion, done. I would look for again a very
similar small type of range movement, and I will tip my hand as to what I think might happen on Twitter. I
won't be doing a live session in the last hour. But I want you to do this homework assignment.

section_title: DRAWDOWN MITIGATION
content:
And again, this is going to seem completely absurd, but I promise you it helps. It helps to understand where
you are in your understanding and learning. But I want you to paper trade the ES and trade with I don't
care how big of a size you make. The purpose is, you want to draw that account from $150,000 of paper
trading account. You want to drop it down $3,000 today. Try not to go more than $3,000, get as close as you
can to $3,000. That way your account should reflect around $147,000. Once you have that, your
assignment is, before Friday's close, you have to get back to $150,000. But you cannot drop down to
$144,000. You can't go there. If you hit $144,000 any time, you failed. Do not be afraid to fail. The whole
purpose is this is as a baseline. It's a baseline measurement because this is the beginning of the year.
We've only been doing this for a few weeks. In the midpoint of the summer, before we get to November,
which is when we end in the mentorship that we're doing here every day, I will do it again, and you're going
to see the ability that you have in November that you don't have right now. And you might have a measured
improvement the second time we do it halfway through. I don't know what exactly that's going to be, but I'm
going to give you the assignment to do the same thing. Do not be afraid. In other words, if you hurt yourself
doing it in the paper trading and you go down below $144,000, you failed, and have no shame in it. And
then you're going to compare what you do the second time you do it in a couple months. And you're
probably going to notice something totally different, which is what you would expect, right? If you learn and
know more, you'll be able to do it better. But I'm purposely choosing this week because it's hard. Exactly
how real-world trading is going to be. You're going to feel like you want to chase big movement. You're
going to see other people talk about, 'Oh, there's going to be movement because the Fed Chairman's
talking.' They all want to be part of these big volatile dates, but big volatile days bring what? Max loss days.
So how do I answer your question of how do I come back from drawdown, how do I overcome fear of
losing, how do I navigate when I'm down, what do I do to fix it? Well, I'm teaching you, but you have to 'ruin
yourself' here in your paper trading account. That way you have it, you see it, and then you have an
experience of trying to fix it based on what you know right now versus what you'll see halfway through this
year when we do the second baseline measurement of your understanding. You might fail the second one
too, and there's no shame in that. But I promise the majority of you won't fail that third one. You're going to
know exactly what you're looking for, you're going to know exactly how to do it, how to engage it, and you
will have conquered the whole fear of drawdown. You won't worry about trying to get it back real quick.
Notice I didn't say, 'Create a pseudo $3,000 drawdown and then try to get it back today.' I didn't say that. I
said 'this week.' That's the proper mindset of correcting drawdown, not 'I hurt myself, I need to fix it right
now, I'm embarrassed, I'm ashamed, and hope nobody asked me what my results were today because I
couldn't be honest and tell them,' because I'd be embarrassed that someone would say 'You're a scrub, you
can't trade,' or whatever because you did something, even you made a mistake, you lost some money.
Every trader loses money. Every trader does it wrong. These are my concepts, and sometimes I try to
finesse it, I try to bring my own ego into it sometimes, and think I'm going to outperform my own stuff, and
then it bites me. That's normal, it's going to happen to you too. There is no perfect trader out there. There's
no perfect system, there's no perfect anything. Anything good placed in the hands of a human being (and
I'm human, obviously, I was sick), but placed in the hands of a human being, the frailty of our humanity will
produce substandard results. And you have to right now come to grips with it, and you treat your trading
education with a new resolve, permitting you as the student, as the trader in development, to be fallible, to
make mistakes. You're going to blow it, you're going to hurt yourself, you're going to do dumb things. You're
going to look at the market, expect it's going to go higher, and it won't, it goes lower, and then you didn't
participate in them. So how do you avoid regretting that or chasing it, knowing that the next moves will
come? See, that's what is missing from most every trader out there that's on social media, chasing people
like me, following me, you're waiting for me to drop a breadcrumb. You're watching other live streamers do
amazing, pushing the button and taking the risk on in front of everyone and making money. I have a great
deal of respect for that, but I'm not trying to do that with you. I want you to be an independent thinker. And
there's nothing wrong with still watching, because I'm an independent thinker, and I watch other live
streamers that do well. I watch other live streamers crash and burn. I don't sit in their comment section or
their chat room and troll them. I'm just observing their reaction to their own decisions, and I'm trying to see
how they perform as the trader. I don't care or even subscribe to any of the methodology they use, even if it
is profitable. I don't have any affinity for any of that. But I take all those experiences by watching traders and
watching the reactions of other folks that are in those chat rooms, and it allows me to, number one, build
lessons for all of you, because I'm basing it on something that I already anticipate by watching traders that
don't have a lot of experience. They want something to occur in price, they want to manifest their
expectation, and they want to impose their will. And that is unfortunately not how this business is won. The
business is won by having an understanding about what is likely to occur, and then looking for evidence to
support that idea, and submitting to it, even if it's uncomfortable. And you have to submit the amount of
time. And time being 10:30 now, it hit our New Week Opening Gap, it traded down into our Opening Range
Gap, which is shaded in that (I don't know what that color would be, like a tangerine color, like an orange, I
don't know what it's called, 'French tan,' whatever). That's Opening Range. It drops down into that and into
a Fair Value Gap and clearing relative equal lows here. At the same time, that the Dow was not willing to
make that lower low comparably. So this is an SMT divergence. So it reflects what? Accumulation of longs.
But you can't just simply take it on its own. You have to start weighing out the risk on risk off, which is like
the rocket fuel behind your trades. If you're going long on ES or going long on NASDAQ, you want to see
that Dollar Index going lower. You do not want to see the Dollar Index rally. You can see it consolidate. It
can consolidate, and the markets can still see the index futures move, but they're going to be quickly
corrected at a later time when that dollar starts moving. Dollar, it is a wonderful instrument to measure if
your trade is going to be extremely efficiently, quickly delivered in terms of your target.

section_title: DXY CURRENT OUTLOOK AND REDEFINING RESISTANCE IN PRICE DELIVERY
content:
If it is not in support of which would be the opposite direction, this entire run here from that Fair Value Gap
up to here, if we look at the Dollar Index, it should be lower. It has been. But now look what we're seeing
with the Dollar Index. See how we're coming off the lows here? Coming off the lows here, straight up in
this, see sell-side balanced by side efficiency. Retail sees this as what? Support broken here, now it should
act as resistance, right? I don't subscribe to that; I just think it's too myopic. The fact that we're in here, we
might trade up to consequent encroachment, have a failed lower low here on the Dollar Index, and then
consolidate a little bit going into lunch. That's how I would interpret it, that's how I'd expect it. Why?
Because I don't think there's a lot of movement that would be reasonable to expect today because of what
we're expecting to see delivered with Fed Chair Powell tomorrow in the morning session. So if you haven't
already noted your calendar or looked at it, that's something you want to do on the weekends before the
trading week even begins. Don't just look at what the calendar is going to be on Monday; look at the entire
weekly calendar. Where are the big heavy stones in terms of high impact or medium impact news drivers?
Is the Fed chair going to be talking? Because if you have those types of things, it's going to dictate how we
have the weekly range delivered to us. It's going to dictate how the daily ranges are formed. Are they going
to be one-sided? Are they going to be slow and high resistance like this is? See, this is not a low resistance
liquidity run, even though it performed like we were outlining, and it's done so nicely. It just takes a whole lot
of time to get there. And I'm not trying to teach you impatience. But the things that you're looking for
through impatience right now, because you're limited in your understanding, the very thing that you're
looking for is a real quick sudden price move that gets you right to your target and you're out. That way, you
don't incur a lot of time holding risk. I understand that; that's exactly why I went into my analysis and
studied these types of situations and conditions, because I as a young man was impatient, extremely
impatient. So I wanted to be in trades that ran away in my favor, got to my targets quickly, and I got out of
the trade so I didn't have to have any risk. And then I had to recuperate because it was very stressful for
me. I'm running a thousand scenarios in my mind: what if it hits my stop? What if it doesn't go to my target?
What happens if it just sits here and it doesn't move tomorrow night because I was holding trades overnight
as a commodity trader? So it was a very daunting task for me because it doesn't fit my personality. I make
decisions really quick. And you might not feel that way as a person or have that personality or that
characteristic as a person; you might be a little bit more slower in terms of making your decisions, and this
trade may be perfect for you. You might look at this and say, 'This is exactly what I'm looking for!' Great,
that's wonderful. I'm not trying to say that you can't find trades in high resistance liquidity run signatures.
You can. I just prefer not to; they're just very boring to me. I don't like them. I like looking for trades like a
word search, or a crossword puzzle. I'm looking for the ones that's going to be very quick, sudden, have a
lot of magnitude and just speed, just runs off. It's exciting, it makes your trading really fun. Whereas this, for
a new student, you probably watch all this stuff, some of you were probably hoping it was going to fail. The
idea of sitting here watching these candles paint, it feels like watching grass grow. But it's not like that when
you're trading in low resistance liquidity runs. It's fast, it's excitement, there's a lot of price movement, it's
just, you'll see. Trust me, right now we're still in this really crummy kind of market condition, but it'll loosen
up. And when it does, you will absolutely know by definition, because you've seen it then, what a low
resistance liquidity run feels like and see what it looks like in your chart. You'll watch it, you'll see how fast it
takes off in speed, it's just like, 'Boom!' Doesn't waste a lot of time, it just immediately wants to go
somewhere. And if you're on board, that is so fun, it's so fun. These types of moves, yes, while profitable,
while delivering to targets that we can see before they deliver them, that's we're not making an argument
against that. And again, I'm not trying to discourage any of you, because if this makes sense to you and
you're comfortable with that, don't let me disparage that because of me saying I like low resistance.
Because a lot of you like to treat me like a brand, like whatever I wear you want to wear, you know, what
cologne I want to wear today, you want to go out and buy it. And to me, it makes me a little uncomfortable.
That's the part of this I don't want to be a celebrity, and I don't want you to look at me like that, and
especially in my trading ideas. Like whatever I like out of my concepts, don't try to frame that as yours.
Because then what you're essentially doing is what I'm trying not to do with you, which is press you into a
mode. If I felt that my concept only worked this way, in that one particular way of doing it, I would say it only
works like this. I don't do that. When I talk about price delivery, I am very dogmatic like that. That's where I
am dogmatic, because if you trade in line with how I teach how price is going to move, even retail stuff will
work. The problem is, retail logic isn't going to lean on the things I'm teaching because they don't subscribe
to the idea that there's something controlling price. Anyway, your assignment today, I'm going to close this
live stream. Your assignment is to go on your TradingView paper trading account. You should...
